name: Conservation of Detail
summary: The narrative principle that details mentioned in a story are significant and will become relevant later, as audiences assume nothing is included without purpose.
explanation:
Conservation of Detail is a narrative principle describing the audience's assumption that every detail presented in a story exists for a reason. If a character's eye color is mentioned, readers expect it to matter. If the camera lingers on a painting, viewers anticipate its significance. This principle is the audience-side corollary of Chekhov's Gun—where Chekhov's Gun instructs writers to remove irrelevant details, Conservation of Detail describes how audiences interpret the details that remain.

The principle is named by analogy with conservation laws in physics—just as energy is neither created nor destroyed, narrative details are assumed to neither appear randomly nor exist without consequence. Every detail 'conserved' in the final narrative carries weight. This creates an implicit contract between storyteller and audience: I will only show you what matters, and everything I show you will matter.

Conservation of Detail explains why audiences can predict plot twists. When a story introduces an unusual detail—a character mentions they can pick locks, the narrative describes a specific window being left open—savvy audiences recognize these as setups. The principle is so ingrained that mystery readers actively track every introduced element, testing each one as a potential clue. This is why skilled mystery writers use red herrings: they exploit Conservation of Detail by introducing purposeful-seeming details that lead nowhere, misdirecting readers who assume everything is significant.

The principle has important implications for different media. In prose, every sentence competes for limited reader attention, so Conservation of Detail is strong—mentioned details feel deliberately chosen. In visual media like film, the camera captures everything in a scene, making selective emphasis (close-ups, lingering shots, rack focus) the mechanism for signaling significance. In video games, interactable objects stand out from the environment, creating a game-specific version of the principle.

Violating Conservation of Detail—including prominent details that never matter—frustrates audiences because it breaks the implicit contract. The story trained them to pay attention, then wasted that attention. However, some genres deliberately violate the principle. Slice-of-life narratives include details precisely because they're mundane and unremarkable. Literary fiction may prioritize atmosphere over plot significance. These genres establish different contracts with their audiences.

For writers, Conservation of Detail is both a constraint and a tool. The constraint: don't clutter your narrative with irrelevant details. The tool: you can plant information in plain sight by disguising it among other details that seem equally important. The audience's assumption that everything matters can be used to hide the truly important detail among several plausible candidates.

references.title: Law of Conservation of Detail - TV Tropes
